title: 🚀 GitHub Commands Cheat Sheet
text:
Think of Git like a digital camera. Making changes in your text editor changes the scenery, but you still need to take the photo and upload it to the cloud.
Here are the four commands you will use every single day to save your work and update your live website.

title: 🚀 GitHub Commands Cheat Sheet
text:
The Daily Publishing Workflow
Whenever you finish a paragraph, add a data table, or update your settings, run these four commands in your terminal in this exact order:

1. Stage Your Changes (The “Pack the Box” Step)
Tell Git which modified files you want to prepare for saving. The period . means “grab every single change in this folder.”
git add .


2. Commit Your Changes (The “Label the Box” Step)
Lock in your save point with a short, descriptive message explaining what you did. Always wrap your message in quotation marks!
git commit -m "Started the Week 1 literature review"


3. Push changes to Github
“Push” or send your changes to Github to make sure your computer matches what is on your Github storage!
git push


4. Publish to the Web (The “Ship the Box” Step)
This magical Quarto command automatically compiles your files into smooth web code and pushes them straight to your live URL.
quarto publish gh-pages

title: 🚀 GitHub Commands Cheat Sheet
text:
Troubleshooting/FAQ
❓ “I ran quarto publish gh-pages but my changes aren’t showing up on my link!”
Did you save your file? Look at the top tab of your file in VS Code or RStudio. If you see a solid circle or dot next to the filename, your file has unsaved changes! Press Ctrl + S (Windows) or Cmd + S (Mac) and try publishing again.
Give it 60 seconds. Sometimes the GitHub servers take a minute to reload your fresh code. Hold down Shift and click the refresh button on your browser to clear your computer’s temporary memory cache.
❓ “My terminal says fatal: not a git repository”
This means your terminal is looking at the wrong folder on your computer (like your desktop instead of your project folder).
Go to the top menu of your editor and select File > Open Project (RStudio) or Open Folder (VS Code) and make sure you explicitly clicked on your my-research-project folder. Then open a fresh terminal.
❓ “Git is asking for a username or password/token!”
Don’t panic! It just wants to verify you own the repository.
If a window pops up asking you to sign in via your browser, click Authorize.
If it asks for your password directly in the terminal, remember that you must use your GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT), not your normal GitHub login password!

title: Introduction
text:
2 Official Research Questions
Your project must be guided by clear, measurable data science inquiries. Use the format below to define your scope.

Primary Research Question (RQ1): e.g., To what extent can historical local weather patterns predict weekend public transit delays in major urban centers?
Secondary Research Question (RQ2): e.g., Does this predictive relationship hold true across different socio-economic neighborhoods within the city limits?

text:

Academic Scaffolding

title: Data Science Corps Exemplar
text:
CREATE PROJECT WEBSITE quarto create-project –type website
INITIALIZE GIT git init
ADD REMOTE CHANGES git add . git commit -m “initial commit”
PUSH REMOTE CHANGES git remote add origin https://github.com/your-username/my-research-project.git git branch -M main git push -u origin main
GO LIVE ON GITHUB PAGES quarto publish gh-pages

title: Do Now: Wednesday, June 24th
text:
Complete these four precise steps immediately upon taking your seat to make sure we’re all starting from the same place!


Things you may need to open:

IDE: RStudio or VS Code. Make sure you open the correct research-project folder (File –> Open Project/Folder –> my-research-project)
Web Browser: Open your github repository, your github pages url, AND this Do Now page (np767.github.io/MDI_Project/do_now.html/)




Run the following command in the terminal once you have opened your project folder:
git status
🙋CHECKPOINT: Look at the output in your terminal.

If it says On branch main or On branch master, proceed to Step 3.
If it says fatal: not a git repository, or anything else, raise your hand and I will come and help you.




Re-publish your page to make sure all changes/updates are live. Run the following command exactly in the terminal:
quarto publish gh-pages
Once it publishes, refresh your page once or twice to make sure no links are broken, and all your work is accurate and updated.



Today is a “bridge” day. Before we can download datasets, we must lock in our research goals. Your milestones for today’s lab session are:

Literature Review: Complete your reading and summarize AT LEAST 1 target background paper on your homepage. Update your index.qmd file to reflect your summary and findings.
Research Questions: Narrow your target scope down to AT LEAST 1 Primary question. Update your index.qmd file to reflect your research question(s).
Locate Data: Identify AT LEAST 1 exact CSV link, API, or database you will analyze.
Start the Data Tab: Create a new file in your project folder called data.qmd. Update your data.qmd file to document your data source as well as a brief summary of the contents of the data. Feel free to copy my template (by finding my data.qmd file inside the modules folder in my repository), or create your own with custom Markdown!







Important: How to add the Data tab to your navigation menu





Because data.qmd is a brand new page, you need to tell your website to show it in the top navigation bar. When you get to this step, your data tab will not show up if you re-publish your page. Raise your hand and let me know when you have reached this step!

title: Creating a Website with Quarto
text:
Before we can start creating our website with Quarto, we need to make sure we have the necessary programs and packages installed. Here are the steps to set up your environment: 1. Install R: If you haven’t already, download and install R from the CRAN website.

Install RStudio: RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) for R. You can download it from the RStudio website.
Install Python: If you plan to use Python for data processing or other tasks, you can download and install Python from the Python website. Make sure to add Python to your system PATH during installation.
Install Jupyter Notebook: If you want to use Jupyter Notebooks for your project, you can install it using pip:
pip install notebook
Install Quarto: You can download and install Quarto from the Quarto website. Follow the installation instructions for your operating system.
Install Required R Packages: Open RStudio and install the necessary packages for working with Quarto. You can use the following command to install the quarto package:

install.packages("quarto")

Install Additional Packages: Depending on your project, you may need additional packages for data manipulation, visualization, or other tasks. You can install them using the install.packages() function in R. For example, if you need the ggplot2 package for data visualization



Some of the things we installed in the previous section (like Python and R) are programming languages, while others (like Jupyter Notebook and Quarto) are programs that allow us to write and execute code in those languages. To keep our project organized and avoid conflicts between different projects, it’s a good idea to set up a virtual environment for our project.
A virtual environment (like venv for Python or renv for R) is a self-contained “sandbox” that contains all the necessary packages and dependencies for your project. Using a virtual environment helps to avoid conflicts between different projects and ensures that your project has access to the correct versions of packages.



Sandbox

title: Creating a Website with Quarto
text:
Working Directories
Your working directory is the folder where your current active files in your directory tree are. When you run commands in your terminal or execute code in your Quarto project, it will look for files relative to this working directory. It’s important to keep track of your working directory, especially when referencing files in your project.
To practice mapping paths, replace the example path below with the absolute path to your new project folder on your machine (e.g., C:/Users/YourName/... on Windows or /Users/YourName/... on Mac).


Code
import os

# EXERCISE: Change the string below to match YOUR local computer path
os.chdir('/Users/nikhilpatla/Documents/DSAN/MDI/Week_1')

# Check the current working directory to confirm it worked
print("Current working directory:", os.getcwd())


Current working directory: /Users/nikhilpatla/Documents/DSAN/MDI/Week_1


Your working directory should be set to the root of your project, where your main .qmd files and _quarto.yml configuration file are located. This way, when you reference files in your project (like images or scripts), they will be found correctly.
This allows you to maintain a clean and organized project structure, making it easier to manage your website as it grows!
